reviews.review_msg: Miremad is a great prof! Very passionate and accessible outside of class. Ask him many questions as he is very helpful in guiding you down the right path. I learned a lot of useful things from him in office hours and during his seminars. This class was fun.
He's just random. There is a final exam for the course, but he never explains what it will be like. Yes, he shared some questions, but it feels like a fluff class. I don't know, I learned nothing.

The group project was very vague! 3 pages (1 page was just the data dictionary) of instructions about the group project. One group in the class just assumed a bunch of stuff and apparently that is what Miremad looks for. You have been warned.

Miremad is an outstanding educator in the field of customer analytics. He possesses a rare gift for conveying complex analytical and statistical concepts with exceptional clarity and strong intuitive reasoning. His teaching methodology transforms non-straightforward material into engaging and comprehensible lessons.

MY GOAT. HE TALKS SO MUCH BS WITH SO MUCH CONFIDENCE DURING THE CLASS THAT YOU'LL QUESTION IF YOU ARE WRONG, NOT BEING ABLE TO UNDERSTAND IT. HE'S IN GOD COMPLEX, ONE THING I WOULD WANT TO LEARN IS TO TALK LIKE HIM AND BE A MULTI LEVEL MARKETING GENIUS. GOD BLESS. THANK YOU FOR YOUR ATTENTION TO THIS MATTER. GOD BLESS SFU.

- AN SFU STUDENT.

I dislike this course because of the prof. He is friendly and easy to approach. In his tutorial, he glazes chatGPT a lot. We had weekly quizzes from readings. Final exam was very long. I received no feedbacks from my assignment, and the professor is disorganized. Some parts of his rubric are weird. Take a STATS course if you want to learn more.

He is very approachable, knowledgeable, passionate, and kind. Show up to class, do the weekly reading, and study the materials he focuses on, then you would do great and enjoy it, as he brings many valuable and applicable real examples. I'm surprised to see the low ratings; they are from students with non-analytical background. I recommend him 100%

The prof is hard to follow and lacks effective teaching skills. Group work consists of weekly tutorial assignments (just 2 or 3 days to complete), a presentation, and a heavy end of sem assignment. Plus, there are weekly quizzes. Very time-consuming for just 3 credits. Avoid this prof if you can but all profs seem not great for this course.

Took Miremad for 343 & now again for 445. It's clear how passionate he is about analytics b/c he is so enthusiastic about his 445 class, it's really great to see. Passionate about what he's teaching, so it's easy to ask him questions. He is a great guy & professor, but the nature of the course and concepts can be difficult to some students

Professor Yang is really helpful outside lecture hours and is really friendly as well. She answers outside of class hours fairly quickly. The main issue is that the class moves really quick and her slides don't really explain much. She uses another's prof's format/structure so there's little room for error. The content is really interesting though.
Tough course but Yupin did a good job explaining concepts and was extremely helpful. Her accent can make it tough to understand her at first, but she paces a difficult course well!

I was excited to take this class, but was very disappointed with Yupin's teaching. I am sure she is very knowledgeable, but her ability to teach students (who are new to R-studio) is below standard. Her lectures feel all over the place - it seems like she is always second guessing herself while speaking it's frustrating. I would not recommend her!

Probably the biggest workload course I've had at SFU. She apparently uses a different profs structure so she doesn't give any room to breathe. There are weeks where she assigns multiple things that are due in very close time frames. It feels as though she disregards all other courses students are taking and forces you to shift all focus to hers.

While Yupin is a great person, the majority of her lectures are unrelated to her assignments. Her lectures explain the theoretical side of things but do not help at all with the application. All the graded assignments and quizzes don't really relate to her lectures and you're left to learn on your own costing marks. Averages were low for the class

All the assignments are based off a software called R Studio. It's a fairly difficult software to use for first timers. The issue is that the lectures do not teach you anything about how to use R Studio and you're kind of just thrown into the water and forced to figure out how to use it yourself. Having no lab makes this class very hard.

Dr. Krider adapted this course to online very well. Weekly labs/assignments/readings were reasonable length and well connected. The R programming was well explained and shouldn't scare you away. The marking was timely and fair.

My favourite prof at SFU so far (by far). He is engaging, has witty humour, and knows his stuff. The course isnt easy, reading through the tutorials and weekly readings is a MUST. Weekly quizzes are based on readings, and fair. 2 large projects, dont underestimate the time these will take. He's a fair grader, but you will need to work for marks.

Not sure why the previous poster feels that way. While not the most dynamic speaker, he gets the point across well enough. The class structure is fairly repetitive in that material is repeated in the text, lecture and lab, but it helps reinforce your skills. Probably the least demanding 400-level class. No surprises on assignments or exams.

Useless professor. I dont have a single good thing to say about him. If you want to take this class i suggest a different professor Jason Ho would be 50 times better. This guy doesnt know a thing about the class he teaches. Useless

He's great! Very knowledgeable and engaging. His class is one of the few classes at Beedie, that I would say really have important real-world application, and not just concepts and keywords to throw out there.  He's also super helpful in tutorials, and really cares about the students. Very organized. A bit goofy, and endearing.
